content:
Artificial Intelligence

LLM

Chrome Extension

JavaScript

Leetcode

5 min read
Just now

--

A Chrome Extension that reads a LeetCode problem, sends it to an AI model, generates a solution, and injects the code directly into the Monaco editor.

Why I Built This
Like every Computer Science student, I spend a lot of time solving LeetCode problems for interviews.
While practicing, I noticed something interesting.
Whenever I got stuck, my workflow always looked like this:

• Read the question

• Open ChatGPT or another AI assistant

• Copy the entire problem statement

• Paste it

• Wait for the response

• Copy the generated code

• Go back to LeetCode

• Paste it inside the editor

• Then review it
It wasn’t difficult.
It was just repetitive.
So I thought…
Why not automate the boring part?
That idea became my Chrome Extension:
LeetCode Auto Solver
Instead of manually copying everything, the extension performs the repetitive workflow automatically while still allowing the user to review the generated solution before submission.

What Does It Do?
The extension can:

• Detect the current LeetCode problem

• Extract the title

• Read the complete problem statement

• Detect the selected programming language

• Read the starter code from Monaco Editor

• Send everything to an OpenAI-compatible LLM

• Receive the generated solution

• Inject the solution back into the editor
Optionally submit the solution
It also includes several quality-of-life features like:

• Daily Problem discovery

• Random Problem discovery

• Local history logs

• Configurable AI providers

• Automatic solving triggers

• JSON export for debugging

Tech Stack
The project is built entirely as a Manifest V3 Chrome Extension.
Frontend

• HTML

• CSS

• Vanilla JavaScript
Chrome APIs

• chrome.storage

• chrome.tabs

• chrome.runtime

• chrome.scripting

• chrome.alarms
AI Providers

• Groq

• OpenRouter

• OpenAI

• Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint

Project Architecture
User
│
▼
Chrome Extension Popup
│
▼
Content Script
│
Reads Problem + Monaco Code
│
▼
Background Service Worker
│
▼
LLM API Request
│
▼
AI Generated Solution
│
▼
Inject into Monaco Editor
│
▼
Optional Auto SubmitEverything runs inside the browser.
There is no backend server.

Manifest V3
The project uses Manifest Version 3.
{
"manifest_version": 3,
"name": "LeetCode Auto Solver",
"permissions": [
"activeTab",
"storage",
"tabs",
"scripting",
"alarms"
]
}Manifest V3 improves security while allowing background service workers to manage API communication efficiently.

Extracting the Problem
The content script is responsible for reading the active LeetCode page.
Instead of relying on only one DOM selector, multiple fallbacks are used because LeetCode occasionally changes its UI.
Example:
function readTitle() {
const selectors = [
"[data-cy='question-title']",
"div.text-title-large",
"h1"
]; for (const selector of selectors) {
const text = document.querySelector(selector)?.textContent?.trim();
if (text) return text;
}
}The extension also extracts:

• URL

• Statement

• Selected language

• Starter code

Reading Monaco Editor
One challenge was accessing the Monaco Editor.
Instead of scraping visible code, the extension communicates with the page and retrieves the editor content directly.
The extracted information is then packaged as:
{
title,
url,
language,
statement,
starterCode
}This object becomes the AI prompt.

Sending Data to the AI
The background service worker handles API communication.
Instead of hardcoding a single provider, I designed the extension to work with multiple OpenAI-compatible APIs.
Simplified request:
fetch(endpoint, {
method: "POST",
headers: {
Authorization: `Bearer ${apiKey}`,
"Content-Type": "application/json"
},
body: JSON.stringify(payload)
});This makes the extension flexible enough to work with:

• Groq

• OpenRouter

• OpenAI

• Local models

• Self-hosted APIs

Injecting the Generated Solution
Once the response arrives, the extension automatically writes the code into Monaco Editor.
Workflow:
Read Problem↓Generate Prompt↓Send to LLM↓Receive Code↓Inject into EditorThe user can still inspect and modify the generated solution before deciding to submit.

Daily & Random Problem Discovery
Another feature I enjoyed building was automatic problem discovery.
Instead of opening LeetCode manually, users can click:

• Daily Problem

• Random Problem
The extension fetches problem metadata and opens the selected challenge directly.

Local History
For debugging and tracking usage, every important event is stored locally.
Examples include:

• Problem extraction

• Solution generation

• Auto-run events

• Submission status
This makes debugging significantly easier while developing new features.

Automatic Triggers
The extension also supports automation features.
Examples include:

• Auto solve after spending a certain number of minutes on a problem.

• Daily streak reminders.

• Maximum auto-solves per day.
These options are configurable from the extension settings.

Biggest Challenges
During development, I encountered several challenges:
1. Dynamic DOM
LeetCode frequently updates its interface.
Using multiple selectors and fallback strategies helped improve reliability.

2. Monaco Editor
Injecting code into Monaco isn’t as straightforward as modifying a textarea.
The extension communicates with the editor in a more controlled manner to insert generated code correctly.

3. Manifest V3
Migrating logic to a background service worker required rethinking message passing between:

• Popup

• Content Script

• Background Worker

4. API Flexibility
Instead of locking users to a single provider, I implemented configurable endpoints so users can choose whichever OpenAI-compatible API they prefer.

What I Learned
Building this project taught me much more than writing JavaScript.
I gained practical experience with:

• Chrome Extension Development

• Manifest V3

• Content Scripts

• Background Service Workers

• Chrome Messaging APIs

• DOM Manipulation

• Monaco Editor Integration

• REST APIs

• Prompt Engineering

• Browser Storage

• LLM Integration

Future Improvements
Some features I’d like to add in the future include:

• Multi-language explanations

• Complexity analysis

• AI-generated hints instead of full solutions

• Code optimization suggestions

• Test case visualization

• Support for additional coding platforms

• Better offline support using local language models

Final Thoughts
This project started as a small idea to remove repetitive steps from my coding workflow.
What began as a simple automation experiment eventually became a full Chrome Extension capable of integrating browser automation, DOM extraction, AI inference, and editor manipulation into one seamless experience.
More importantly, it helped me understand how modern AI applications can be embedded directly into everyday developer tools rather than existing as separate websites.
If you’re interested in Chrome Extensions, browser automation, or AI integration, building a project like this is an excellent way to learn all three at once.

Disclaimer
This project was created for educational purposes, browser extension development practice, and learning how to integrate Large Language Models (LLMs) into web applications.
AI-generated code may contain mistakes and should always be reviewed before use. Users are responsible for ensuring that any generated solutions comply with the terms of service, honor codes, and contest rules of platforms such as LeetCode. This extension is intended to assist learning and reduce repetitive tasks — not to encourage academic dishonesty or unfair use in competitive programming or technical assessments.

GitHub
If you’d like to explore the source code, feel free to check out the GitHub repository and share your feedback or suggestions. Contributions and improvements are always welcome!
